coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with superior sensitivity, stability and molecular recognition capability are highly desirable for sensing applications in complex environments. Herein, a new strategy to fabricate confined CQDs within PANI-derived polymer dots is designed, based on solvothermal treatment of polyaniline (PANI) and 2,3-diaminonaphthalene (2,3-DAN) using N,N-dimethylformamide as the solvent. The obtained hybrids demonstrates a unique stable “lotus-pod” structure, of which PANI-derived polymer dots functions as the pod room to confine the in situ grown CQDs. The hybrid exhibits dual-emission (blue-orange), and the existence of quinoid moieties in CQDs enables ultrasensitive fluorescent recognition of aniline with a detection limit of 12.46 pM. Its selectivity towards aniline is also outstanding in comparison of other aromatic interferents with similar structures and functional groups. The sensing mechanism could be owing to the interaction between the quinoid nitrogen and aniline molecule, resulting in fluorescence quenching. This work provides a novel design strategy to construct well-confined CQDs embedded in polymer dots with excellent sensing ability for promising fluorescent sensing applications, especially under complex conditions.

head.ce:title: Dual-emissive polyaniline derived lotus-pod nano-hybrids for ultrasensitive and highly selective fluorescent recognition towards aniline

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with superior sensitivity, stability and molecular recognition capability are highly desirable for sensing applications in complex environments. Herein, a new strategy to fabricate confined CQDs within PANI-derived polymer dots is designed, based on solvothermal treatment of polyaniline (PANI) and 2,3-diaminonaphthalene (2,3-DAN) using N,N-dimethylformamide as the solvent. The obtained hybrids demonstrates a unique stable “lotus-pod” structure, of which PANI-derived polymer dots functions as the pod room to confine the in situ grown CQDs. The hybrid exhibits dual-emission (blue-orange), and the existence of quinoid moieties in CQDs enables ultrasensitive fluorescent recognition of aniline with a detection limit of 12.46 pM. Its selectivity towards aniline is also outstanding in comparison of other aromatic interferents with similar structures and functional groups. The sensing mechanism could be owing to the interaction between the quinoid nitrogen and aniline molecule, resulting in fluorescence quenching. This work provides a novel design strategy to construct well-confined CQDs embedded in polymer dots with excellent sensing ability for promising fluorescent sensing applications, especially under complex conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Aniline, as key intermediates in chemical, food and pharmaceutical industries, have been classified into the Class III carcinogen by the World Health Organization International Agency for Research on cancer. It was highly toxic, which could cause acute poisoning and liver damage [1]. Therefore, it is of the utmost importance to develop effective sensors to detect low concentration of aniline for quick warning. Compared to several techniques such as solid-phase micro-extraction method, the fluorescence sensing method have been verified as an easily-operational and low-cost technique, exhibiting the most easily detectable responses to amine recognition [1–5]. In spite of prominent progress, the sensing of liquid anilines still remains challenging, considering urgent demands for fluorescent sensors of superior sensitivity, stability and molecular recognition capability in complex environments.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new emerging functional carbon-based material, have been widely utilized in the field of chemical sensing and biosensing [6–9]. The peculiar quantum confinement effect and rich surface groups of CQDs have endowed them colorful and tunable fluorescence. Tremendous researches have reported the fluorescent sensing performance of CQDs towards gaseous or liquid organic pollutants, including aldehydes, benzene derivative and organic dyes [6,10]. However, it could not be ignored that the CQDs are easily aggregated when exploited in a complex environment, due to the large surface area and active surface groups [11,12]. The aggregation behaviors of CQDs would influence the fluorescence property, thus hampering furtherly practical sensing applications [13]. Besides, the molecular recognition capability is also a vital indicator in measuring the sensing performance, while this property was rarely studied in most fluorescent sensing works towards aniline based on CQDs [1].

Great efforts have been devoted to improve the stability of carbon dots through embedding carbon dots in inorganic (e.g. silica, zeolites, layered double hydroxides (LDHs), etc.) or organic matrices (e.g. urea, polyurethane, poly (vinyl alcohol), etc.) [14,15]. Although the employment of these substrates was supposed to generate typical room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) emission of carbon dots, the obtained hybrids also contribute to create a matrix-assisted stable structure, relying on the stabilization strategies of hydrogen bonds, covalent bonds and structural confinements [14,16,17]. However, most fabricated structures tightly fixing the CQDs in their three-dimensional (3D) matrix networks would inhibit “molecular capture”, conversely influencing the sensitivity. Thus, open platforms tailoring the CQDs are highly desired to achieve effective breakthroughs on both stability and sensitivity.

Among these matrixes, polyaniline (PANI) is an excellent choice due to its facile and low-cost synthesis, extended π-conjugated system, good redox reversibility and stability in solutions [18,19]. Various CQD-PANI hybrid systems have been developed, and potential applications have been investigated in the fields of energy-storage devices (pseudocapacitors, supercapacitors, solar cells) and electrochemical (EC) detection [20–22]. Furthermore, synthesis of carbon dots directly utilizing PANI as a single source via hydrothermal route has also been studied, which showed as an electrochemical sensor for 2,4,6-trinitrophenol [23]. Notably, these applications mainly take advantages of the superior electrical property of PANI, while the fluorescence sensing performance of the hybrids is seldomly reported. Actually, the key features of CQDs and PANI could both satisfy the principles of fluorescent sensing via proper doping and size modulation [24–26].

In this work, a strategy is exploited to fabricate confined CQDs within PANI-derived polymer dots of high stability and excellent molecular recognition capability. The design of hybrids is inspired by the lotus-pod structure, of which the pod room serves as a stabilization platform for seeds. During the solvothermal reaction, PANI is utilized as the main precursor and 2,3-diaminonaphthalene (2,3-DAN) as the organic ligand. Our results demonstrate that the obtained bionic structure exhibits dual-emission, showing a unique lotus color under the excitation of 365 nm. The mechanism of dual-emission is studied via the structural and morphology analysis, by comparison of the influence of the organic ligands. More importantly, the hybrid show excellent sensitivity towards aniline. Its detection limit toward aniline is 1.16∗10−3 μg/L (12.46 pM). Eight kinds of aromatic interferences have negligible effects on the sensitivity, and four other aromatic compounds with similar structures and functional groups as that of aniline only quench part of the fluorescence. Therefore, this work provides an effective strategy to improve the stability and sensitivity of CQDs for fluorescence sensing of aniline.

The polyaniline was prepared via the traditional interfacial polymerization method [27]. Firstly, aniline and ortho-xylene were mixed in a volume ratio of 1:1. Then, ammonium persulfate (APS) was dissolved in HCl (1 mol/L). 2 mL of aniline/ortho-xylene mixture was dropped slowly into the APS/HCl solution and the whole system was stirred for 2 h. After stirring, the solution turned into dark green with the formation of slurry. The dark green slurry was washed by deionized water for several times, and dried at 80 °C for 24 h. The final green dark bulk solid was fully grind into powder for further experiment.

The PANI-derived lotus-pod nano-hybrids were synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal method. Firstly, 50 mg PANI powder and 50 mg 2,3-Diaminonaphthalene (2,3-DAN) were dissolved in DMF (30 mL) under sonication for 30 min. Then, the mixture were transferred into 50 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 8 h. After reaction, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm Millipore membrane to remove large-sized impurities. The filtered solution was dark brown and dialyzed for 48 h utilizing a dialyze bag with cut-off of 500 Da. The dialyzed solution in the bag was then dried and 45 mg black product was obtained which was named as PD. Besides, three parallel trials were carried out to compare the influence of different precursors. When the precursor was 50 mg PANI powder, the obtained products was named as P, while when 50 mg 2,3-DAN, the product was named as D. When the precursor was 50 mg PANI and 50 mg 1,8-diaminonaphthalene (1,8-DAN), the obtained products was named as PD-2.

The purified PD was dissolved in DMF to obtain a dispersion with a concentration of 0.2 mg/L. Then, different volumes of aniline solution was added into the dispersion, and the quantitative estimation of aniline was obtained via measuring the PL intensity. The stability and selectivity of the sensor was studied by measuring the PL spectra towards a series of analytes and pollutants.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku D/Max 2550 X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) with Cu-Ka radiation (γ = 0.15418 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out with a ESCALAB 250 Xi system (Thermo Scientific, USA). All binding energies were calibrated using the carbonaceous C 1s line at 284.6 eV as reference. Raman spectra were carried out by using a HR 800 Raman Microscopy (Horiba, France) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) experiment was carried out on a Nicolet IS50 spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The samples were prepared as pellets together with KBr. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were performed on a Tecnai G20 electron microscope (FEI, USA) equipped with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. The UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was taken on a UV-580 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy was performed using a FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The variable temperature fluorescence was measured using a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were collected with Edinburgh FLS980 steady state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The synthetic scheme of PD was shown in Fig. 1 a. The morphology and structure of PANI prepared via the interfacial polymerization method were demonstrated in Fig. S1. The morphology of PANI observed via SEM showed that stacked-sheet nanoscale structures occurred in a stirred reaction, which was similar with that reported by Li et al. [27]. XRD pattern exhibited an obvious diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.2°, corresponding to the (200) plane of PANI. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1258, 1391 and 1669 cm−1 shown in the FT-IR spectrum of PANI could be attributed to stretching vibrations of C–N, CC and CN bonding, respectively.

To study the influence of reaction temperature and time on the yield and optical properties of PD, several parallel trials were carried out, as shown in Fig. S2. It was found that the yield achieved the highest for reaction at 180 °C for 8 h, and the final PD solution displayed a special lotus color under irradiation at 365 nm. Its PL spectra showed a strong emission peak at ∼380 nm and a weak broad peak around 565 nm in Fig. 1b. The sharp peak at 365 nm was owing to the excitation wavelength. The influence of temperature on the optical properties was shown in Figs. S2a and b. With the increase of temperature, three samples obtained at 150, 180 and 200 °C all showed lotus color, but their PL intensities gradually decreased. In their UV–vis spectra, two peaks at 322 and 340 nm shown in all samples could be attributed to the intra-ligand π–π∗ transition of the CN bonds and the n-π∗ transition of CO bonds, respectively [28,29]. Besides, there is a special peak at 290 nm in sample-150 and sample-180 which could be attributed to the typical π-π∗ transition along the backbone of the PANI chain. This peak vanished in sample-200 [30,31]. The results indicated that thermal oxidative decomposition of the PANI polymer chain occurred and the carbonization degree of the final sample increased with the increase of reaction temperature. The influence of temperature on the optical properties was shown in Figs. S2c and d. With the proceeding of the reaction, the PL intensities gradually decreased. Meanwhile, the absorbance peak at 290 nm slowly disappeared, while the peak at 322 nm became obvious. These phenomenon displayed the collapse of polymer skeleton and over-carbonization process under long reaction time. Thus, the yield of the sample achieved the highest after reacting for 8 h.

The structural changes were also testified via FT-IR spectra, as shown in Fig. S3. The characteristic absorption bands of samples were assigned in Table S1, according to the literatures [20,32]. For sample-PD reacted for 8 h at 180 °C, typical peaks owing to the primary PANI skeleton included absorption peaks around 1403, 1109, 868 and 743 cm−1, assigned to the phenazine ring stretching, N = Q = N/C–H in-plane deformation, C–H out-of-plane bending vibrations on a 1,4-disubstituted benzene ring and C–H out-of-plane deformation of monosubstituted or 1,2-disubstituted ring. Other peaks observed around 1644, 1471, and 617 cm−1 corresponded to N–H scissoring of primary aromatic amine/phenazine, bending vibrations of NH groups, and deformations of benzene ring. By comparison, the bands of sample-PD-150 °C, sample-PD-1h and sample-PD-2h owned a weak absorption peak at 868 cm−1 and lost the characteristic peak at 743 cm−1. The two peaks were both assigned to the typical phenazine ring stretching. Then the intensity of the two bands declined sharply for sample-PD-200 °C and sample-PD-16 h, while retained the typical absorption of aromatic benzene rings. These analysis results furtherly confirmed the collapse of polymer skeleton and over-carbonization process.

To elucidate the origin of different emission peaks, the dialyzed solution was treated with solvents of different polarities to distinguish the components. When the solution was diluted with benzene, a polar precipitate was collected, named as B-PD. When the solution was diluted with water, a non-polar precipitate was collected, named as Y-PD. After re-dissolving the precipitates in DMF solvent, the B-PD exhibited blue emission while Y-PD orange-yellow. Their PL spectra shown in Fig. 1b corresponded exactly to the short- and long-wavelength emission part of that for PD. Meanwhile, in their UV–vis spectra (Fig. 1c), the B-PD exhibited two peaks at 322 and 340 nm, while Y-PD exhibited the typical peak at 290 nm. The peaks matched well with that of the original PD sample. These results implied that the final PD was indeed a composite of two components, where the polar B-PD emit blue emission and non-polar Y-PD emit orange-yellow emission. Two other sets of experiments studying the precursor ratio was furtherly carried out, and the PL QYs of all the products obtained under different reaction conditions were then calculated, provided in Table S2, according to the methods introduced in Reference [25]. Based on the product yield and PL quantum yield, the reaction condition (8 h, 180 °C, the ratio of 2,3-DAN: PANI as 5:5) was chosen as the optimized condition.

The morphology was studied via TEM images, as seen from Fig. 1d–f and Fig. S5. Interestingly, it was found that sample-PD exhibited as nano-hybrids. Shown in Fig. S5, dispersed nanoparticles could be observed. Interestingly, when a particle was irradiated by the electron beam for a while, the amorphous part began to vanish and a colony of crystalline dots became obvious (Fig. 1d). The lattice fringe of 0.21 nm corresponded to the (100) plane of graphene, indicative of the species of CQDs [6]. Two more cases were provided in Figs. S5d–f. Furtherly, the morphology of B-PD and Y-PD were inspected. It was shown that B-PD were also well-crystalline CQDs with an average size of ∼6 nm. The Y-PD were large amorphous nanoparticles which were similar with most reported polymer dots [33]. Thus, it could be suspected that PD was a “lotus pod”-like structure, in which the polar CQDs of blue emission was the “lotus seed” and the nonpolar polymer dots of orange-yellow emission was the “lotus seedpod”. The CQDs might be embedded into the polymer dots via weak van der Waals' force rather than strong chemical bonding, since they could be separated via solvent treatment.

To find out the structural difference, FT-IR spectra of the three samples were obtained, as shown in Fig. S6. The characteristic absorption bands of sample-PD were assigned in Table S1, according to the literatures [18,34]. All the typical peaks assigned to the primary PANI skeleton could be observed in the IR spectrum of sample-PD. By comparison, the bands of Y-PD was similar with that of PD. It owns most of the typical bonding species of PANI and PANI-derived materials. For sample B-PD, it exhibited an obvious peak at 1080 cm−1, besides of the peak at 1109 cm−1. The new peak could be assigned to the C–O stretching vibrations. The disappearance of this peak in sample-PD might be owing to that the CQDs were embedded in the polymer dots via interaction with the C–O bonds.

To clarify the function of precursors, the optical properties, morphology and structure of sample-P, D, PD (2,3-DAN as the precursor) and PD-2 (1,8-DAN as the precursor) were characterized. It could be seen from Fig. 2 a that sample-D exhibited the same emission at ∼380 nm as sample-PD while lacked the long-wavelength emission. The difference was also reflected on their UV–vis spectra of which sample-D displayed two absorption peaks at 322 and 340 nm, and the absorption peak at 290 nm was not observed, as shown in Fig. 2b. For sample-P, both PL and UV–vis spectrum differed greatly from that of sample-PD. In our work, sample-P displaying two obvious peaks at 411 and 438 nm denoted the polaronic band to π band transition and the de-excitation of polaron band in PANI-derived structures, respectively [35]. Besides, the broad absorbance band at 500–600 nm for sample-P corresponded to the inter ring charge transfer of benzenoid to quinoid moieties [31].

Moreover, 1,8-DAN, the isomer of 2,3-DAN, was utilized as the precursor to discuss the influence of the organic ligand. Their molecular structures were depicted in the inset of Fig. 2b. Obviously, sample PD-2 exhibited very weak fluorescence under the same irradiation condition as other samples. The PL intensity of sample PD-2 have been enlarged via increasing the slit width. It was then found that sample PD-2 lost the typical characteristic of dual emission, by showing a weak broad PL emission at ∼415 nm. Its UV–vis spectrum exhibited a red shift in absorption at 334 nm and 350 nm. These results indicated the importance of the structure of 2,3-DAN in triggering short-wavelength PL emission, which might be owing to the chemical activity of amino groups located at 2,3 positions of naphthalene ring [32,36]. The TEM images of sample-P and sample-D were then observed, shown in Fig. S7. The sample-P demonstrated typical polymer nanoparticles of amorphous structure, and sample-D were aggregated carbon quantum dots with lattice fringe of 0.21 nm. The distinct optical properties suggested the reaction between PANI and 2,3-DAN leaded to the special dual emission and final lotus PL color.

The structures of the four samples were studied via Raman, XPS and FT-IR spectra. Their Raman spectra showed little difference, as seen from Fig. S8a. The ID/IG of sample-PD was the smallest which might be due to higher percentage of conjugated structure. According to the XPS spectra shown in Fig. S8b, the contents of carbon atoms in sample-P, sample-D, sample-PD and sample-PD-2 were 26.34%, 23.07%, 78.45% and 78.05%, respectively. The N atoms were 3.80%, 1.52%, 13.79% and 15.26%, respectively. The O atoms were 69.86%, 75.41%, 7.77% and 6.70%, respectively. Due to the introducing of organic molecules in the reaction, the amount of carbon and nitrogen specifies was greatly increased in sample-PD and PD-2, while the absorbed oxygen specified dropped sharply. Further high resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s spectra (Fig. 2c) then provided detailed information about different bonding species. By comparison of the C 1s spectra, it was found that the four samples all showed the typical bond species, which represented C–C/CC (284.6 eV), C–N (285.5 eV) and CN (287.2 eV). Another very weak peak at 288.5 eV in sample-P could be assigned to the OC–O [36,37].

The analysis of N 1s spectra provided more effective evidences of structural differences, listed in Table 1 . Their deconvoluted peaks varied from each other in four samples. In sample-P, D and PD, a same peak with the binding energy of 389.8 eV was found, which could be owing to the quinoid amine (–N = ). For sample PD-2, a peak with the binding energy of 398.2 eV was found, generally assigned to pyridinic nitrogen. Another two peaks in sample-D and PD-2 were assigned as amino nitrogen (399.4 eV) and graphite nitrogen (401 eV), respectively. These peaks mainly appeared in the XPS spectra of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, which remained consistent with the TEM images. For sample P and PD, the peak centered at 399.8 eV was then assigned as benzenoid amine (–NH–). Another peak at 401 eV might correspond to nitrogen cationic radical (–NH+–) [38–40]. Moreover, it was found that the percentage of quinoid amine was higher in sample-D and sample-PD. The quinoid amine was proven to be an important fluorescent bond in the following sensing experiments. For oxygen species, the percentage breakdown of different bonds within the O 1s XPS high-resolution spectra was listed in Table S3. Four samples all showed the typical bond species of O–C O (532.2 eV) and C–O–H (532.8 eV), respectively [41]. However, the high-resolution O 1s spectra of PD and PD-2 differed obviously from that of P and D, showing one extra peak at 531.5 eV [42]. This new peak could be assigned to CO, indicative of that the reaction between PANI and diaminonaphthalene leaded to the formation of this bond.

The FT-IR spectra in Fig. 3 were also consistent with the XPS analysis results. The spectra of sample-PD nearly covered that of sample-P and -D. Notably, the band at ∼1109 cm−1 in sample P, which was attributed to N = Q = N/C–H in-plane deformation of PANI-derived structure, distinctly shifted in sample PD and PD-2. This peak was used to measure the degree of delocalization of the electrons, and the shift might be caused by the coordination at the site or the van der waals kind of interaction between PANI-derived structure and CQDs, which have been reported in previously reported literatures [22,43]. On the other hand, there was an obvious strong band in sample D showing around 1270 cm−1, which could be assigned to the C–N stretching of secondary aromatic amine. This band was also observed in sample PD, but not in sample PD-2, accentuating the effect of 2,3-amino positions in the condensation process. Besides, another peak at 1094 cm−1 in sample-D which could be ascribed to the C–O stretching was consistent with that in sample B-PD. The FT-IR analysis results verified the interaction between B-PD and Y-PD, and the importance of amino sites in diaminonaphthalene during the reaction.

To furtherly understand the fluorescence origins, the PL emissions of sample-PD under different pH values were measured, as seen from Fig. 4 a and b. It was found that PD emitted stable and even stronger emissions in alkaline environment, while the PL peak at 380 nm sharply decreased when the environment was changed from alkaline to acid. Besides, the PL emission shifted to longer wavelength at 405 nm, and the solution displayed blue color under irradiation at 365 nm, shown in the inset of Fig. 4b. The UV–vis spectra of sample-PD in acidic and alkaline environment were then performed, shown in Fig. S9. It was found that the absorption edge of the doped and dedoped sample-PD were very distinct. The absorption edge shifted to the right obviously in acidic environment while shifted to the left slightly in alkaline environment, which indicated the decrease of bandgap for the doped state and the increase of bandgap for the dedoped state. The observed changes in the optical bandgap corresponded well to the shift in the emission wavelength during the doping/dedoping process. Based on the above structural characteristics, the pH-triggered fluorescence switching mechanism was shown in Fig. 4c. According to the above-mentioned FT-IR analysis results, CQDs possessed the structure of benzenoid and quinoid rings, resulting relatively localized molecular orbitals. Upon proton doping, the difference between the benzenoid and quinoid structures was decreased via an intra-chain charge transfer process. Thus, the polaron and bi-polaron orbitals became more delocalized, leading to a lower energy gap between ground state (S0) and excited state (S1) [25,44,45]. This phenomenon has also been studied in some previously reported literatures [25,44,45].

For aniline sensing, two previously reported works have demonstrated the preparation of carbon dots aerogel for aniline gas sensing [3,4]. Another work showed the liquid aniline sensing performance of carbon dots with a LOD of 3.57 nM without studying the molecular recognition ability towards aniline [5]. Depending on the composition and structure of sample-PD, it was speculated that its fluorescence sensing ability toward liquid aniline would be comparable to or even better than those used in the standard methods. The sensing abilities of sample-PD, P and D were firstly studied, shown in Fig. 5 a. It was found that sample-PD exhibited the best sensing ability toward aniline, and obvious fluorescence weakening could be observed even at a low concentration of 3 μg/L.

Meanwhile, a wide dynamic range from 0.01 to 300 μg/L was achieved by sample-PD at the concentration of 0.2 mg/L, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity showed a good linear relationship, as seen from Fig. 5b. For sample-PD, the emission peak at 344 nm was owing to the excitation wavelength, while the display of emission peak at ∼373 nm is due to the existence of the vibrational fine structure in PL spectra. Vibrational fine structures of the fluorescence band could be influence by the solvent environment, leading to the variation of intensity [46]. Thus, the emission peak at 380 nm was selected to calculate the LOD. The loading condition of ∼0.2 mg/L was found to the optimal choice based on the detection optimization by examining different loading concentrations of sample-PD. The sensing performances in case of higher (∼2 mg/L) and lower loading condition (∼0.05 mg/L) were shown in Fig. S10. It was found that higher loading leaded to high background signal, thus weakening the sensing performance in the lower range of detection. Meanwhile, lower loading would limit the upper range of detection, since it was easily quenched by higher concentration of aniline. Besides, the effect of the solution pH on the sensing of aniline was studied for pH 8, 10 and 12, shown in Fig. S11 [47]. The sensitivity of sample-PD towards aniline in acidic environment was not measured, since the emission peak of sample-PD shifted obviously. It was found that the F/F0 increased very slightly with the increase of the alkalinity, which might be due to the slight increase of fluorescence intensity at higher alkaline pH values. Thus, the pH value of 8 was chosen. On the other hand, sample Y-PD was also studied for aniline sensing since it owned the typical quinoid moieties. The experimental results in Fig. S12 showed that the fluorescence of Y-PD could be quenched by aniline. However, due to the weak fluorescence of Y-PD, it could be easily quenched by low concentration of aniline, which would limit the upper range of detection. The linear regression equation was calculated as ln(F/F0) = -0.02718 × C-0.25354 (R2 = 0.99454). Based on the equation and 3σ rule (σ is the standard deviation of the blank solution), the limit of detection (LOD) toward aniline was estimated to be 1.16 × 10−3 μg/L (12.46 pM). Notably, the addition of aniline did not change the emission maxima of sample-PD, implying that no exciplexes were formed during the quenching process [1]. The LOD in this work was also compared with the reported results, listed in Table S4. Obviously, our LOD was of strong competitiveness when aniline was in liquid status, and comparable to some results when aniline was in gaseous status.

To assess the precision of sensing, two indexes of reproducibility and repeatability were examined, and the relative standard deviation (RSD%) of the mean recovery was utilized as seen from Table 2 . According to the ICH guidelines, the reproducibility is defined as the measurements of different analytics concentrations with lab-variations by different analysts at different elapsed time, while the repeatability is defined as replicated measurements of different analytics concentrations in the same lab via the same analyst and equipment within the day [48]. Herein, the reproducibility tests were conducted again after two days from the fresh examination, and completed within a week for three days by two different analysts. Meanwhile, three different aniline concentrations of 3, 10 and 30 μg/L were repeatedly measured. The data summarized in Table 2 all showed recovery exceeding 98%, proving that the obtained PD probes is an excellent choice for aniline sensing [5,48].

Besides the sensitivity, reproducibility and repeatability, anti-interference ability was also very important to assess the sensing ability of sample-PD towards aniline. Herein, some aromatic compounds with similar structure, including chlorobenzene, o-dichlorobenzene, phenol, hydroquinone, benzoic acid, terephthalic acid, trimesic acid, 2-cyanoguanidine, m-sulfanilic acid and glycine were explored. Their molecular structures were listed in Fig. S13. As shown in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence of sample-PD was nearly quenched with the addition of aniline (100 μg/L), while the additions of other aromatic compounds (10 mg/L for each) displayed little effect on the fluorescence intensity of sample-PD. Moreover, the influence of different ions were also studied. The results showed that 10 mg/L stock solutions of each of Na+, K+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, NH4 +, Cl−, Br−, NO3 − barely quench the fluorescence, and the addition of Fe3+ slightly decreased the PL intensity, as seen from Fig. 5d.

Furtherly, the interference of the mixtures of these organic compounds and ions were examined. Shown in Fig. S14, the mixtures of these metal ions (10 mg/L for each) slightly quench the fluorescence mainly due to the existence of Fe3+, while the addition of 50 μg/L aniline into the mixture obviously leaded to the decrease of PL intensity. Meanwhile, the mixtures of various aromatic compounds (10 mg/L for each) also barely quench the fluorescence, while the addition of 35 μg/L aniline into the mixture obviously leaded to the decrease of PL intensity. Meanwhile, the influence of ionic strength was also investigated via adjusting the species of ions. Ionic strength represents the variation of activity coefficient with concentration in electrolyte solutions. Four different salts mixtures were then prepared, which contained different types of cationic and anionic ions. The concentration of each ion was 10 mg/L. The PL emission peaks of sample-PD (blank), and the mixtures of sample-PD and different salts were shown in Fig. S15. It was found that there was a weak influence of ionic strength on the fluorescence of sample-PD.

During the experimental process, it is worth mentioned that 500 mg/L stock solutions of Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+ ions could induce obvious fluorescence quenching. Hundreds of works have demonstrated utilization of carbon dots for Fe3+/Al3+/Cr3+ metal ions sensing. Generally, the fluorescence of carbon dots could be easily quenched by the three metal ions via the coordination action based on the reported mechanisms [49–51]. Considering the structural characteristics of sample-PD, it was supposed that the metal ions would chelate with the oxygen or amine moieties to alter the non-radiative transition process, furtherly quenching the fluorescence of sample-PD. In details, Fe3+ ion can interact with the amine groups on the surface of the sample-PD, and the electrons in the excited state would transfer to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching [49]. Al3+ with more positive charges and smaller radius is thought as hard acid. The oxygen of CO bonding on CDs’ surface could be regarded as hard base, and then combined strongly with Al3+ on basis of the rule of hard and soft acids and bases, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching [50]. The quenching mechanism of Cr3+ was similar with that of Fe3+, since the surface nitrogen moieties could bind Cr3+ utilizing coordination chemistry [51]. To shield the interfering influence of the three metal ions, new sensing experiments were then carried via adjusting the pH of the solution. As shown in Fig. S16, the fluorescence of the sample-PD could be quenched by Fe3+, Al3+ and Cr3+. However, after adjusting the alkalinity of the solution, the fluorescence all recovered. After adding aniline into the solution, fluorescence quenching could be observed again. Therefore, these data revealed that our method is interference-free towards many organic and inorganic species.

To identify the molecule recognition capability of sample-PD more precisely, its sensing ability towards some other aromatic compounds with similar structures and functional groups as that of aniline were then explored, shown in Fig. 5e–f. It was found that polyaniline could quench part of the fluorescence, which might be owing to the π–π interactions between the lotus-pod structures and the precursors. Notably, sample-PD demonstrated obviously different sensing performance towards the three isomers of C6H4(NH2)2 (o-PD, m-PD and p-PD). The addition of p-PD in sample-PD caused a distinct fluorescence quenching compared to the other two species. The different quenching degrees indicated that the site of amino groups in benzene-structure played a key role in influencing the PL. Meanwhile, 4-bromoaniline and 4-chloroaniline could not lead to fluorescence quenching. The phenomenon might be owing to the different alkalinity of –NH2 groups. It was known that the alkalinity of –NH2 groups gradually decreased in the order of p-PD, aniline, 4-chloroaniline and 4-bromoaniline, demonstrating decreasing electron density around the amine groups. Thus, the alkaline amine groups in p-PD and aniline are more easily interacted with the electron-deficient quinoid group in CQDs [52]. On the other hand, the steric effect could not be ignored, thus the small molecule of aniline could more easily quench the fluorescence of sample-PD. For 2,3-DAN, the quenching mechanism might also be attributed to the π–π interactions between the lotus-pod structures and the precursors, since the π-electron density of naphthalene is higher than that of single benzene ring. To distinguish the quenching species, UV–vis spectra was also measured, and it was found that the absorption peaks varied from each other after adding aniline, 2,3-DAN and p-PD in sample-PD, shown in Fig. S17. Therefore, the three species could be furtherly distinguished based on the UV–vis absorption peaks. The excellent selectivity of sample-PD towards aniline was a result of the combination of amino-reactivity and steric hindrance.

A sensitive and selective sensor is specifically needed for the sensing of aniline in real samples [47,53]. Then, two real samples were chosen for comparison. The pond water and wastewater were both taken. After filtering and centrifugation, the supernatants were utilized without further treatment. The fluorescence of the real samples were firstly measured. It was found that the fluorescent peaks were complex, while the fluorescent intensity was much weaker than that of sample-PD at the concentration of 0.2 mg/L. Then our sample-PD nanoprobe was utilized for determination of aniline in the real sample, and the emission peaks of sample-PD varied little after mixing with the real samples. The accuracy of PD-based sensor in real samples was examined via the mean percentage recovery and relative error calculation. Table 3 shows that the PD sensing systems achieves a decent recovery of 94.97%–106.54% at different aniline concentrations ranging from 2 to 25 μg/L for pond water, while a decent recovery of 98.55%–102.50% for wastewater. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of the aniline sensing systems ranged from 0.15 to 2.52 for real samples. These data demonstrated that the PD-based nanoprobe perform well in terms of the accuracy despite the complex interferences of unknown inorganic and organic compounds in the real samples.

Finally, the fluorescent intensities of three samples stored in the dark for different days were compared in Fig. S19, which showed a slight decrease of PL intensity throughout half-a-year period (storing time: sample 1 for 46 days, sample 2 for 94 days, and sample 3 for 156 days). Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of a freshly prepared sample remained stable throughout one-week period. These results proved that sample-PD was an excellent choice when applied in some complex environments, based on its superior sensitivity, reproducibility, repeatability, anti-interference ability and stability.

To elucidate the quenching mechanism and understand the sensing specificity, the fluorescence properties of sample-PD were furtherly studied via the variable temperature fluorescence and PL decay. Fig. 6 a showed the variable-temperature fluorescence emission spectra of sample-PD with the temperature range varying from 293 K to 373 K. The sample-PD solution demonstrated temperature-dependent PL behavior. With the increase of temperature from 293 K to 353 K, the PL intensity slowly decreased while jumped greatly when the temperature was 373 K. The decreasing PL intensity was generally owing to the enhanced non-radiative recombination due to the temperature enhancement [54]. In Fig. 6b, the PL decay lifetime gradually decreased with the increase of aniline concentration, consistent with the fluorescence quenching. The average fluorescence lifetime was fitted well with the double exponential function using the Edinburgh Instruments F980 software [55], with a lifetime τ of 37.13 ns for PD, 23.82 ns for PD-30, and 15.13 ns for PD-100 in Table S5. The PL dynamics are indeed a combination of trap-assisted (early time) and non-trap-assisted (slower time) charge-carrier recombination [26,56]. It was found that with the addition of aniline molecules, the residence time of the electrons in the excited state was shortened, thus leading to the gradual fluorescence quenching [57]. The structural, optical properties and morphologies of sample-PD before and after adding aniline were then characterized.

The morphology of sample-PD barely changed, shown in the inset of Fig. 6c. Some polymer dots were aggregated, but the CQDs were still well-dispersed, as seen from Fig. S20. However, the UV–vis spectra exhibited the disappearing of the absorbance peak at 322 nm while retaining of the peak at 340 nm. The phenomenon provided direct evidence that aniline influenced the fluorescence of sample-PD via interacting with the CN bonds. Based on previously reported works, hydrogen bonding could be easily formed between the quinoid nitrogen and the hydrogen atom of amino group in aniline [1,44,58]. The interaction could cause the intermolecular electron or proton transfer, thus quenching the fluorescence [59]. The FT-IR spectra were then analyzed to furtherly prove the formation of hydrogen bonding, shown in Fig. 6d. The typical N–H stretching and aromatic C–H stretching among 3500–2800 cm−1 remained unchanged. Nevertheless, another three peaks at 1644 cm−1 (1), 1471 cm−1 (2) and 1270 cm−1 (3) all blue shifted, which were related to the N–H scissoring, N–H bending and C–N stretching vibration modes in the benzenoid ring system, respectively. The blue shift of the three IR peaks verified the existence of hydrogen bonding interaction, which could be attributed to the shrinkage of N-related bonds [60]. These structural analysis results also confirmed the key role of quinoid moieties in molecular recognition, which emitted 380 nm UV light.

According to the above-mentioned analysis, the sensing mechanism of sample-PD was depicted in Fig. 7 . In our design, the PANI and 2,3-DAN were chosen as the precursors to prepare a lotus-pod nano-hybrid. The hybrid were formed via the interaction between polar CQDs and nonpolar polymer dots based on hydrogen bonding. It was suspected that the superior sensibility, stability and specificity of sample-PD towards aniline sensing was owing to the synergistic effect of the special lotus-pod structure and composition. In the hybrid, the polymer dots functioned as a stable platform to support the CQDs, and the embedded CQDs functioned as the sensing “hands” to recognize the aniline molecules. The polymer dots could improve the stability, while the well-dispersed CQDs could improve the sensibility. The sensing mechanism was owing to the interaction between the quinoid nitrogen and aniline molecule, resulting in fluorescence quenching. The CQDs “hands” based on the quinoid moieties also exhibited superior selectivity towards aniline, which have been proven in the UV–vis spectra and IR spectra.

In summary, a dual-emissive hybrid of CQDs embedded within PANI-derived polymer dots is fabricated via an in-situ growth route. The hybrid exhibits a unique “lotus-pod” structure, in which the polar CQDs of blue emission was the “lotus seed” and the nonpolar polymer dots of orange-yellow emission was the “pod room”. In the hierarchical structure, the polymer dots function as an open platform to confine and stabilize the CQDs via weak van der Waals' force, while the CQDs serve as the “catcher” for further aniline sensing. The hybrid exhibits ultrasensitive aniline sensing ability, with a detection limit as low as 12.46 pM. The high recognition accuracy of the hybrid furtherly validate the practicalities. Based on the structural, optical properties and morphology analysis, the quinoid moieties in CQDs are proven to be the key unit in molecular recognition. These findings provide design insights to fabricate bionic structure of good stability and sensitivity for potential applications in molecular fluorescence recognition, especially under complex environments.
